Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=矢奈比売神社 |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|磐田市|Q386651}}見付1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=矢奈比売大神 |社格={{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |札所等 = |創建=不詳 |本殿={{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名=見付天神 |例祭={{jalink|旧暦|Q11511066}}{{jalink|8月10日|Q2779}}直前の土曜・日曜（見付天神裸祭）{{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= 見付天神裸祭 |website= 磐田市公式ウェブサイト |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|一の鳥居}}]] '''矢奈比売神社'''（やなひめじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|磐田市|Q386651}}見付にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|式内小社}}で、旧{{jalink|社格|Q10444029}}は旧{{jalink|県社}}。'''見付天神'''（みつけてんじん）の通称で知られる。 === 祭神 === === 主祭神 === *'''矢奈比売大神''' 『{{jalink|神名帳考証|Q11588824}}』によると、八野若日女命とされている。 === 配祀 === *{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005|菅原大神}} *{{jalink|火之迦具土神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|表筒男神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|中筒男神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|底筒男神}} === 歴史 === 創建年代は不詳。文献上の初見は『{{jalink|続日本後紀|Q1041301}}』{{jalink|承和 (日本)|Q1075605|承和}}7年（{{jalink|840年|Q29936}}）6月に従五位下の神階を授けるという記事であり、{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}2年（{{jalink|860年|Q24714}}）{{jalink|1月17日|Q2264}}には正五位上を授かった。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』には小社に列している。 {{jalink|正暦|Q1324858}}4年（{{jalink|993年|Q24080}}）には、{{jalink|太宰府天満宮|Q710786}}より{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005|菅原大神}}を勧請したといい、以後は「見付の天神様」「見付天神」と呼ばれ崇敬された。{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}8年（{{jalink|1603年|Q6735}}）には、{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より神領50石を寄進された。{{jalink|東海道五十三次|Q3134262}}の宿場町の一つである{{jalink|見附宿|Q782144}}の鎮守とされていた。 === 霊犬悉平太郎伝説 === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|霊犬悉平太郎銅像}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|霊犬神社}}]] この伝説は、当社やその他各地で語られているものであるが、その内容は類型的である。また、悉平太郎（しっぺいたろう）の名は、伝わる地方により異なり、悉平太郎の出身地である{{jalink|光前寺|Q11388300}}では早太郎（はやたろう）と呼ばれる。 見付の里では、毎年8月の初めになると、どこからともなく飛んで来た「白羽の矢」が、ある家の屋根に突き刺さっており、矢を立てられた家は年番といい、年番はその家の娘を生きたまま柩に入れて、8月10日の真夜中に見付天神へ供え、供えられた娘は、生贄として地響きと共に現れた怪神によって食い殺されるという恐ろしいしきたりがあった。これを破ると田畑が荒れ里が凶作に苦しむことになるため里人は泣く泣くこのしきたりを守っていた。 {{jalink|延慶 (日本)|Q1327068|延慶}}元年（{{jalink|1308年|Q5723}}）8月、{{jalink|比丘#雲水|Q854997|雲水}}と呼ばれる旅の僧が見付の宿へ通りかかったところ、この祭りに遭遇し、楽しいはずの祭りなのに里人が悲しい表情をしていることから、話を伺ったところ、この祭りを知ったとされる。見付の里の鎮守であるはずの神様がそのような悪行をするはずがないと訝った雲水が、祭りの夜にその正体を確かめようと神社に向かい身を潜めていると、現れたのは神ではなく恐ろしい怪物であり、「信濃の国の悉平太郎に知らせるな。今宵今晩このことは、悉平太郎に知らせるな」と言いながら柩ごと娘をさらって去っていった。 雲水は怪物を打ち破るため、怪物の恐れる悉平太郎を求めて{{jalink|信濃国|Q289250}}へ向かうが、そのような名前の人物は見当たらず、更に根気よく探していると、それは伊那の光前寺で飼われている犬であることが分かった。雲水は住職に事情を話し、借受の許可をいただくと、悉平太郎を連れて見付に戻った。 見付に戻った頃には翌年の8月になっており、この年も年番の屋根に白羽の矢が立っており、雲水は里人と相談し、娘の代わりに悉平太郎を柩に入れて供えたところ、悉平太郎は現れた怪物と一夜に渡って激しく戦い、見事退治に成功した。怪物の正体は歳を経た猿の妖怪「{{jalink|狒々|Q4497985}}」であった。見付の里人は、悉平太郎の立派な働きぶりに心から感謝し、大般若経六百巻を書き写して光前寺へ奉納した。 境内北部にある'''霊犬神社'''（れいけんじんじゃ）では、霊犬悉平太郎が祀られている。磐田市のPRキャラクター「{{jalink|しっぺい|Q17212154}}」は、悉平太郎をモデルとしたものである。 悉平太郎の出身地である光前寺は現在の{{jalink|長野県|Q127877}}{{jalink|駒ヶ根市|Q838684}}に位置し、この話が縁となり、 {{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}42年（{{jalink|1967年|Q2648}}）{{jalink|1月12日|Q2248}}から磐田市と駒ヶ根市は友好都市関係となっている。 === 境内 === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|二の鳥居 File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|入口石標 === 文化財 === === 重要無形民俗文化財（国指定） === * 見付天神裸祭 - {{jalink|2000年|Q1985}}{{jalink|12月27日|Q2800}}指定 ** 矢奈比売神社の祭神が{{jalink|遠江|Q11642126}}の{{jalink|総社|Q1107129}}である{{jalink|淡海國玉神社|Q43594535}}へ渡御する神事を中心とする祭で、旧暦8月10日直前の土曜・日曜に開催される。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|光前寺|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|遠江国の式内社一覧|Q11642130}} === 外部リンク === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ 見付天神 矢奈比賣神社] -（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=矢奈比売神社 |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|磐田市|Q386651}}見付1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=矢奈比売大神 |社格={{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |札所等 = |創建=不詳 |本殿={{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名=見付天神 |例祭={{jalink|旧暦|Q11511066}}{{jalink|8月10日|Q2779}}直前の土曜・日曜（見付天神裸祭）{{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= 見付天神裸祭 |website= 磐田市公式ウェブサイト |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|一の鳥居}}]] '''矢奈比売神社'''（やなひめじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|磐田市|Q386651}}見付にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|式内小社}}で、旧{{jalink|社格|Q10444029}}は旧{{jalink|県社}}。'''見付天神'''（みつけてんじん）の通称で知られる。 === 祭神 === === 主祭神 === *'''矢奈比売大神''' 『{{jalink|神名帳考証|Q11588824}}』によると、八野若日女命とされている。 === 配祀 === *{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005|菅原大神}} *{{jalink|火之迦具土神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|表筒男神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|中筒男神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|底筒男神}} === 歴史 === 創建年代は不詳。文献上の初見は『{{jalink|続日本後紀|Q1041301}}』{{jalink|承和 (日本)|Q1075605|承和}}7年（{{jalink|840年|Q29936}}）6月に従五位下の神階を授けるという記事であり、{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}2年（{{jalink|860年|Q24714}}）{{jalink|1月17日|Q2264}}には正五位上を授かった。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』には小社に列している。 {{jalink|正暦|Q1324858}}4年（{{jalink|993年|Q24080}}）には、{{jalink|太宰府天満宮|Q710786}}より{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005|菅原大神}}を勧請したといい、以後は「見付の天神様」「見付天神」と呼ばれ崇敬された。{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}8年（{{jalink|1603年|Q6735}}）には、{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より神領50石を寄進された。{{jalink|東海道五十三次|Q3134262}}の宿場町の一つである{{jalink|見附宿|Q782144}}の鎮守とされていた。 === 霊犬悉平太郎伝説 === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|霊犬悉平太郎銅像}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|霊犬神社}}]] この伝説は、当社やその他各地で語られているものであるが、その内容は類型的である。また、悉平太郎（しっぺいたろう）の名は、伝わる地方により異なり、悉平太郎の出身地である{{jalink|光前寺|Q11388300}}では早太郎（はやたろう）と呼ばれる。 見付の里では、毎年8月の初めになると、どこからともなく飛んで来た「白羽の矢」が、ある家の屋根に突き刺さっており、矢を立てられた家は年番といい、年番はその家の娘を生きたまま柩に入れて、8月10日の真夜中に見付天神へ供え、供えられた娘は、生贄として地響きと共に現れた怪神によって食い殺されるという恐ろしいしきたりがあった。これを破ると田畑が荒れ里が凶作に苦しむことになるため里人は泣く泣くこのしきたりを守っていた。 {{jalink|延慶 (日本)|Q1327068|延慶}}元年（{{jalink|1308年|Q5723}}）8月、{{jalink|比丘#雲水|Q854997|雲水}}と呼ばれる旅の僧が見付の宿へ通りかかったところ、この祭りに遭遇し、楽しいはずの祭りなのに里人が悲しい表情をしていることから、話を伺ったところ、この祭りを知ったとされる。見付の里の鎮守であるはずの神様がそのような悪行をするはずがないと訝った雲水が、祭りの夜にその正体を確かめようと神社に向かい身を潜めていると、現れたのは神ではなく恐ろしい怪物であり、「信濃の国の悉平太郎に知らせるな。今宵今晩このことは、悉平太郎に知らせるな」と言いながら柩ごと娘をさらって去っていった。 雲水は怪物を打ち破るため、怪物の恐れる悉平太郎を求めて{{jalink|信濃国|Q289250}}へ向かうが、そのような名前の人物は見当たらず、更に根気よく探していると、それは伊那の光前寺で飼われている犬であることが分かった。雲水は住職に事情を話し、借受の許可をいただくと、悉平太郎を連れて見付に戻った。 見付に戻った頃には翌年の8月になっており、この年も年番の屋根に白羽の矢が立っており、雲水は里人と相談し、娘の代わりに悉平太郎を柩に入れて供えたところ、悉平太郎は現れた怪物と一夜に渡って激しく戦い、見事退治に成功した。怪物の正体は歳を経た猿の妖怪「{{jalink|狒々|Q4497985}}」であった。見付の里人は、悉平太郎の立派な働きぶりに心から感謝し、大般若経六百巻を書き写して光前寺へ奉納した。 境内北部にある'''霊犬神社'''（れいけんじんじゃ）では、霊犬悉平太郎が祀られている。磐田市のPRキャラクター「{{jalink|しっぺい|Q17212154}}」は、悉平太郎をモデルとしたものである。 悉平太郎の出身地である光前寺は現在の{{jalink|長野県|Q127877}}{{jalink|駒ヶ根市|Q838684}}に位置し、この話が縁となり、 {{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}42年（{{jalink|1967年|Q2648}}）{{jalink|1月12日|Q2248}}から磐田市と駒ヶ根市は友好都市関係となっている。 === 境内 === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|二の鳥居 File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|入口石標 === 文化財 === === 重要無形民俗文化財（国指定） === * 見付天神裸祭 - {{jalink|2000年|Q1985}}{{jalink|12月27日|Q2800}}指定 ** 矢奈比売神社の祭神が{{jalink|遠江|Q11642126}}の{{jalink|総社|Q1107129}}である{{jalink|淡海國玉神社|Q43594535}}へ渡御する神事を中心とする祭で、旧暦8月10日直前の土曜・日曜に開催される。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|光前寺|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|遠江国の式内社一覧|Q11642130}} === 外部リンク === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ 見付天神 矢奈比賣神社] -（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=矢奈比売神社 |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|磐田市|Q386651}}見付1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=矢奈比売大神 |社格={{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |札所等 = |創建=不詳 |本殿={{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名=見付天神 |例祭={{jalink|旧暦|Q11511066}}{{jalink|8月10日|Q2779}}直前の土曜・日曜（見付天神裸祭）{{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= 見付天神裸祭 |website= 磐田市公式ウェブサイト |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|一の鳥居}}]] '''矢奈比売神社'''（やなひめじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|磐田市|Q386651}}見付にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|式内小社}}で、旧{{jalink|社格|Q10444029}}は旧{{jalink|県社}}。'''見付天神'''（みつけてんじん）の通称で知られる。 === 祭神 === === 主祭神 === *'''矢奈比売大神''' 『{{jalink|神名帳考証|Q11588824}}』によると、八野若日女命とされている。 === 配祀 === *{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005|菅原大神}} *{{jalink|火之迦具土神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|表筒男神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|中筒男神}} *{{jalink|住吉三神|Q1114013|底筒男神}} === 歴史 === 創建年代は不詳。文献上の初見は『{{jalink|続日本後紀|Q1041301}}』{{jalink|承和 (日本)|Q1075605|承和}}7年（{{jalink|840年|Q29936}}）6月に従五位下の神階を授けるという記事であり、{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}2年（{{jalink|860年|Q24714}}）{{jalink|1月17日|Q2264}}には正五位上を授かった。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』には小社に列している。 {{jalink|正暦|Q1324858}}4年（{{jalink|993年|Q24080}}）には、{{jalink|太宰府天満宮|Q710786}}より{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005|菅原大神}}を勧請したといい、以後は「見付の天神様」「見付天神」と呼ばれ崇敬された。{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}8年（{{jalink|1603年|Q6735}}）には、{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より神領50石を寄進された。{{jalink|東海道五十三次|Q3134262}}の宿場町の一つである{{jalink|見附宿|Q782144}}の鎮守とされていた。 === 霊犬悉平太郎伝説 === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|霊犬悉平太郎銅像}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|霊犬神社}}]] この伝説は、当社やその他各地で語られているものであるが、その内容は類型的である。また、悉平太郎（しっぺいたろう）の名は、伝わる地方により異なり、悉平太郎の出身地である{{jalink|光前寺|Q11388300}}では早太郎（はやたろう）と呼ばれる。 見付の里では、毎年8月の初めになると、どこからともなく飛んで来た「白羽の矢」が、ある家の屋根に突き刺さっており、矢を立てられた家は年番といい、年番はその家の娘を生きたまま柩に入れて、8月10日の真夜中に見付天神へ供え、供えられた娘は、生贄として地響きと共に現れた怪神によって食い殺されるという恐ろしいしきたりがあった。これを破ると田畑が荒れ里が凶作に苦しむことになるため里人は泣く泣くこのしきたりを守っていた。 {{jalink|延慶 (日本)|Q1327068|延慶}}元年（{{jalink|1308年|Q5723}}）8月、{{jalink|比丘#雲水|Q854997|雲水}}と呼ばれる旅の僧が見付の宿へ通りかかったところ、この祭りに遭遇し、楽しいはずの祭りなのに里人が悲しい表情をしていることから、話を伺ったところ、この祭りを知ったとされる。見付の里の鎮守であるはずの神様がそのような悪行をするはずがないと訝った雲水が、祭りの夜にその正体を確かめようと神社に向かい身を潜めていると、現れたのは神ではなく恐ろしい怪物であり、「信濃の国の悉平太郎に知らせるな。今宵今晩このことは、悉平太郎に知らせるな」と言いながら柩ごと娘をさらって去っていった。 雲水は怪物を打ち破るため、怪物の恐れる悉平太郎を求めて{{jalink|信濃国|Q289250}}へ向かうが、そのような名前の人物は見当たらず、更に根気よく探していると、それは伊那の光前寺で飼われている犬であることが分かった。雲水は住職に事情を話し、借受の許可をいただくと、悉平太郎を連れて見付に戻った。 見付に戻った頃には翌年の8月になっており、この年も年番の屋根に白羽の矢が立っており、雲水は里人と相談し、娘の代わりに悉平太郎を柩に入れて供えたところ、悉平太郎は現れた怪物と一夜に渡って激しく戦い、見事退治に成功した。怪物の正体は歳を経た猿の妖怪「{{jalink|狒々|Q4497985}}」であった。見付の里人は、悉平太郎の立派な働きぶりに心から感謝し、大般若経六百巻を書き写して光前寺へ奉納した。 境内北部にある'''霊犬神社'''（れいけんじんじゃ）では、霊犬悉平太郎が祀られている。磐田市のPRキャラクター「{{jalink|しっぺい|Q17212154}}」は、悉平太郎をモデルとしたものである。 悉平太郎の出身地である光前寺は現在の{{jalink|長野県|Q127877}}{{jalink|駒ヶ根市|Q838684}}に位置し、この話が縁となり、 {{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}42年（{{jalink|1967年|Q2648}}）{{jalink|1月12日|Q2248}}から磐田市と駒ヶ根市は友好都市関係となっている。 === 境内 === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|二の鳥居 File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|入口石標 === 文化財 === === 重要無形民俗文化財（国指定） === * 見付天神裸祭 - {{jalink|2000年|Q1985}}{{jalink|12月27日|Q2800}}指定 ** 矢奈比売神社の祭神が{{jalink|遠江|Q11642126}}の{{jalink|総社|Q1107129}}である{{jalink|淡海國玉神社|Q43594535}}へ渡御する神事を中心とする祭で、旧暦8月10日直前の土曜・日曜に開催される。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|光前寺|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|遠江国の式内社一覧|Q11642130}} === 外部リンク === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ 見付天神 矢奈比賣神社] -（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{jalink|Old calendar|Q11511066}} {{jalink|August 10|Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{jalink|Iwata|Q386651}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{jalink|Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Ho-no-Kagutsuchi-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 7 ({{jalink|840|Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}), on {{jalink|January 17|Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} 4 ({{jalink|993|Q24080}}), {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{jalink|Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{jalink|Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|Enkyō}} 1 ({{jalink|1308|Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{jalink|Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{jalink|Shinano Province|Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{jalink|hihī|Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{jalink|Shippei|Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{jalink|Nagano Prefecture|Q127877}} {{jalink|Komagane|Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{jalink|January 12|Q2248}} of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 42 ({{jalink|1967|Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{jalink|December 27|Q2800}}, {{jalink|2000|Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{jalink|Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535}}, the {{jalink|sōja|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{jalink|Kōzen-ji|Q11388300}} *{{jalink|Tenjin faith|Q66890725}} *{{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
Former {{ill||lt=kensha|UNKNOWN}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill||lt=kensha|UNKNOWN}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{ill||lt=Kagutsuchi|UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
