Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|UNKNOWN}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|UNKNOWN}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651|1=Iwata, Shizuoka}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779|1=August 10}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651|1=Iwata, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō Studies}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936|1=840}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714|1=860}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264|1=January 17}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080|1=993}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786|1=Dazaifu Tenmangū}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735|1=1603}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144|1=Mitsuke-juku}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300|1=Kōzen-ji}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō|1=Enkyō (Kamakura period)}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723|1=1308}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui|1=Bhikkhu}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985|1=hihī}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154|1=Shippei}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684|1=Komagane, Nagano}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248|1=January 12}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648|1=1967}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800|1=December 27}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985|1=2000}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535|1=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126|1=Tōtōmi}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300|1=Kōzen-ji}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130|1=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651|1=Iwata, Shizuoka}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779|1=August 10}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651|1=Iwata, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō Studies}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936|1=840}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714|1=860}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264|1=January 17}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080|1=993}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786|1=Dazaifu Tenmangū}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735|1=1603}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144|1=Mitsuke-juku}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300|1=Kōzen-ji}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō|1=Enkyō (Kamakura period)}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723|1=1308}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui|1=Bhikkhu}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985|1=hihī}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154|1=Shippei}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684|1=Komagane, Nagano}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248|1=January 12}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648|1=1967}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800|1=December 27}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985|1=2000}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535|1=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126|1=Tōtōmi}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300|1=Kōzen-ji}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130|1=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Yanahime Jinja Ichinotorii.jpg {{commons category|Category:Yanahime-jinja}} [[JA:矢奈比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q65498694}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Yanahime Shrine |画像=[[File:Yanahime Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|coord={{coord|34|43|47.2|N|137|51|50.7|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651|1=Iwata, Shizuoka}} Mitsuke 1114-2 |ISO = JP | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 43 | 緯度秒 = 47.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 51 | 経度秒 = 50.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神=Yanahime-no-Ōkami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |札所等 = |創建=Unknown |本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名=Mitsuke Tenjin |例祭=Saturday and Sunday immediately before {{ill|lt=Old calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} {{ill|lt=August 10|Q2779|WD=Q2779|1=August 10}} (Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri){{Cite web|url= https://www.city.iwata.shizuoka.jp/sports_midokoro/bunkazai/kunishitei/1002052.html |title= Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri |website= Iwata City Official Website |date= 2023-03-20 |accessdate= 2024-09-07 }} |神事= |地図 = Japan Shizuoka |ラベル位置 = }} [[File:Yanahime_Jinja_Ichinotorii.jpg|250px|thumb|{{center|First torii}}]] '''Yanahime Shrine''' (やなひめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Mitsuke, {{ill|lt=Iwata|Q386651|WD=Q386651|1=Iwata, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed minor shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is commonly known as '''Mitsuke Tenjin''' (みつけてんじん). === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === *'''Yanahime-no-Ōkami''' According to '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō Studies}},' she is identified as Yano-Wakahime-no-Mikoto. === Associate deities === *{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuno-o-no-Kami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} === History === The date of founding is unknown. The earliest literary record appears in '{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}},' which states that in the 6th month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 7 ({{ill|lt=840|Q29936|WD=Q29936|1=840}}), the shrine was granted the divine rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|WD=Q24714|1=860}}), on {{ill|lt=January 17|Q2264|WD=Q2264|1=January 17}}, it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}},' it is listed as a minor shrine. In {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} 4 ({{ill|lt=993|Q24080|WD=Q24080|1=993}}), {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara-no-Ōkami|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} was ritually invited from {{ill|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū|Q710786|WD=Q710786|1=Dazaifu Tenmangū}}, and thereafter the shrine was revered and known as "the Tenjin of Mitsuke" or "Mitsuke Tenjin." In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735|1=1603}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated a divine estate of 50 koku to the shrine. It served as the tutelary shrine of {{ill|lt=Mitsuke-shuku|Q782144|WD=Q782144|1=Mitsuke-juku}}, one of the post stations along the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Gojūsan-tsugi|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}}. === Legend of the Spirit Dog Shippeitarō === {{see also|しっぺい太郎|光前寺#早太郎説話}} [[File:Reiken Shippeitarou Douzou.jpg|200px|thumb|right|{{center|Bronze statue of the spirit dog Shippeitarō}}]] [[File:Yanahime Reiken Jinja.jpg|250px|thumb|right|{{center|Reiken Shrine}}]] This legend is told at this shrine and in various other locations throughout Japan, and its content follows a common pattern. The name Shippeitarō (しっぺいたろう) varies by region; at {{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300|1=Kōzen-ji}}, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, he is called Hayatarō (はやたろう). In the village of Mitsuke, every year at the beginning of August, a white-feathered arrow would come flying from an unknown source and lodge itself in the roof of a house. The household struck by the arrow was designated the "year-duty house," and was required to place a daughter alive into a coffin and offer her at Mitsuke Tenjin at midnight on August 10. The offered maiden would be devoured by a monstrous deity that appeared amid earth-shaking tremors. If this custom was broken, the fields would be ruined and the village would suffer famine, so the villagers tearfully upheld the practice. In the 8th month of {{ill|lt=Enkyō (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkyō|1=Enkyō (Kamakura period)}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1308|Q5723|WD=Q5723|1=1308}}), a wandering monk called an {{ill|lt=Bhikkhu#unsui|Q854997|WD=Q854997|lt=unsui|1=Bhikkhu}} passed through the post station of Mitsuke and encountered this festival. Noticing that the villagers wore sorrowful expressions despite what should have been a joyful occasion, he inquired and learned of the custom. The unsui, doubting that the tutelary deity of Mitsuke could commit such evil, went to the shrine on the night of the festival and hid himself. What appeared was not a deity but a terrifying monster, which chanted, "Do not tell Shippeitarō of Shinano about this. Tonight, tonight, do not let Shippeitarō know of this!" and carried off the maiden together with the coffin. To defeat the monster, the unsui set out for {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}} seeking the Shippeitarō whom the creature feared. He could find no person by that name, but after persistent searching, he discovered that Shippeitarō was a dog kept at Kōzen-ji in Ina. The unsui explained the situation to the head priest, received permission to borrow the dog, and returned to Mitsuke with Shippeitarō. By the time he returned, it was already August of the following year, and once again a white-feathered arrow had appeared on the roof of the year-duty house. The unsui consulted with the villagers, and instead of a maiden, placed Shippeitarō in the coffin as the offering. Shippeitarō fought ferociously through the night against the monster that appeared, and splendidly vanquished it. The monster's true form was a "{{ill|lt=hihī|Q4497985|WD=Q4497985|1=hihī}}," a monkey yōkai of great age. The villagers of Mitsuke expressed their heartfelt gratitude for Shippeitarō's valiant deed and copied all six hundred volumes of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, presenting them to Kōzen-ji. At '''Reiken Shrine''' (れいけんじんじゃ), located in the northern part of the precinct, the spirit dog Shippeitarō is enshrined. The Iwata City PR character "{{ill|lt=Shippei|Q17212154|WD=Q17212154|1=Shippei}}" is modeled after Shippeitarō. Kōzen-ji, the birthplace of Shippeitarō, is located in present-day {{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|WD=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}} {{ill|lt=Komagane|Q838684|WD=Q838684|1=Komagane, Nagano}}. Because of this legend, since {{ill|lt=January 12|Q2248|WD=Q2248|1=January 12}} of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648|1=1967}}), Iwata City and Komagane City have maintained a friendship city relationship. === Precinct === File:Yanahime Jinja Honden.jpg|Honden File:Yanahime Jinja Torii.jpg|Second torii File:Yanahime Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|Entrance stone marker === Cultural properties === === Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (nationally designated) === * Mitsuke Tenjin Hadaka Matsuri - Designated {{ill|lt=December 27|Q2800|WD=Q2800|1=December 27}}, {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985|1=2000}} ** A festival centered on the ritual procession (togyo) in which the deity of Yanahime Shrine proceeds to {{ill|lt=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine|Q43594535|WD=Q43594535|1=Ōmi Kunitama Shrine}}, the {{ill|lt=sōja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q11642126|WD=Q11642126|1=Tōtōmi}}. It is held on the Saturday and Sunday immediately before the 10th day of the 8th month of the old calendar. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === *{{ill|lt=Kōzen-ji|Q11388300|WD=Q11388300|1=Kōzen-ji}} *{{ill|lt=Tenjin faith|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}} *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130|1=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province}} === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} *[https://mitsuke-tenjin.com/ Mitsuke Tenjin Yanahime Shrine] - (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなひめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yanahime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Tenmangū (worship)]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:磐田市の歴史]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]] [[Category:磐田市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]
